concepts.title: Preparation of Haloalkanes
concepts.description: Haloalkanes can be prepared by the reaction of alkanes with halogens (Cl2, Br2) in the presence of light, and by the reaction of alcohols with halogenating agents (PX3, SOC2).

concepts.title: Nucleophilic Substitution Reactions
concepts.description: Nucleophilic substitution reactions of haloalkanes involve the replacement of the halogen atom by a nucleophile, and the reaction proceeds through SN1 or SN2 mechanisms.

concepts.title: SN1 and SN2 Mechanisms
concepts.description: SN1 mechanism is a two-step process involving the formation of a carbocation intermediate, while SN2 mechanism is a one-step process involving a concerted backside attack by the nucleophile.

concepts.title: Remembering SN1 and SN2 Conditions
concepts.description: Use the mnemonic 'SN1 - Secondary, Neutral, 1 step' and 'SN2 - Secondary, Strong, 2 steps' to remember the conditions for SN1 and SN2 reactions.

concepts.title: Confusion between SN1 and SN2 Mechanisms
concepts.description: Students often confuse the SN1 and SN2 mechanisms. To avoid this, remember that SN1 involves a carbocation intermediate and is favored by polar protic solvents, while SN2 involves a concerted mechanism and is favored by polar aprotic solvents.

concepts.title: Physical Properties of Haloalkanes
concepts.description: Remember that the boiling points and melting points of haloalkanes increase with increasing molecular weight and the size of the halogen atom.

concepts.title: Haloarenes Preparation
concepts.description: Haloarenes can be prepared by the reaction of arenes with halogens (Cl2, Br2) in the presence of a catalyst (FeCl3, FeBr3), and by the reaction of arenes with halogenating agents (Cl2, Br2) in the presence of light.

concepts.title: Electrophilic Substitution Reactions
concepts.description: Electrophilic substitution reactions of haloarenes involve the replacement of the halogen atom by an electrophile, and the reaction proceeds through an arenium ion intermediate.
